title: Ensure that Activity Log Alert exists for Create or Update  SQL Server Firewall Rule

input_row.title: Ensure that Activity Log Alert exists for Create or Update  SQL Server Firewall Rule
input_row.description: Create an activity log alert for the Create or Update SQL Server Firewall Rule event.
input_row.rationale: Monitoring for Create or Update SQL Server Firewall Rule events gives insight into network access changes and may reduce the time it takes to detect suspicious activity.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Navigate to the Monitor blade. 2. Click on Alerts. 3. In the Alerts window, click on Alert rules. 4. Ensure an alert rule exists where the Condition column contains Operation name=Microsoft.Sql/servers/firewallRules/write. 5. Click on the Alert Name associated with the previous step. 6. Ensure the Condition panel displays the text Whenever the Activity Log has an event with Category='Administrative', Operation name='Create/Update server firewall rule' and does not filter on Level, Status or Caller. 7. Ensure the Actions panel displays an Action group is assigned to notify the appropriate personnel in your organization. Audit from Azure CLI az monitor activity-log alert list --subscription --query "[].{Name:name,Enabled:enabled,Condition:condition.allOf,Actions:actions}" Look for Microsoft.Sql/servers/firewallRules/write in the output Audit from PowerShell Get-AzActivityLogAlert -SubscriptionId |where-object {$_.ConditionAllOf.Equal -match "Microsoft.Sql/servers/firewallRules/write"}|select-object Location,Name,Enabled,ResourceGroupName,ConditionAllOf Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: b954148f-4c11-4c38-8221-be76711e194a - Name: 'An activity log alert should exist for specific Administrative operations'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Navigate to the Monitor blade. 2. Select Alerts. 3. Select Create. 4. Select Alert rule. 5. Choose a subscription. 6. Select Apply. 7. Select the Condition tab. 8. Click See all signals. 9. Select Create/Update server firewall rule (Server Firewall Rule). 10. Click Apply. 11. Select the Actions tab. 12. Click Select action groups to select an existing action group, or Create action group to create a new action group. 13. Follow the prompts to choose or create an action group. 14. Select the Details tab. 15. Select a Resource group, provide an Alert rule name and an optional Alert rule description. 16. Click Review + create. 17. Click Create. Remediate from Azure CLI az monitor activity-log alert create --resource-group "" --condition category=Administrative and operationName=Microsoft.Sql/servers/firewallRules/write and level= --scope "/subscriptions/" --name "" -- subscription --action-group Remediate from PowerShell Create the Conditions object. $conditions = @() $conditions += New-AzActivityLogAlertAlertRuleAnyOfOrLeafConditionObject - Equal Administrative -Field category $conditions += New-AzActivityLogAlertAlertRuleAnyOfOrLeafConditionObject - Equal Microsoft.Sql/servers/firewallRules/write -Field operationName $conditions += New-AzActivityLogAlertAlertRuleAnyOfOrLeafConditionObject - Equal Verbose -Field level Retrieve the Action Group information and store in a variable, then create the Actions object. $actionGroup = Get-AzActionGroup -ResourceGroupName - Name $actionObject = New-AzActivityLogAlertActionGroupObject -Id $actionGroup.Id Create the Scope object $scope = "/subscriptions/" Create the Activity Log Alert Rule for Microsoft.Sql/servers/firewallRules/write New-AzActivityLogAlert -Name "" - ResourceGroupName "" -Condition $conditions -Scope $scope -Location global -Action $actionObject -Subscription -Enabled $true
input_row.impact: There will be a substantial increase in log size if there are a large number of administrative actions on a server.
input_row.default_value: By default, no monitoring alerts are created.
input_row.justification: The control is based on Azure's CIS benchmark, specifically targeting Azure Activity Log Alerts for SQL Server firewall rule changes. AWS does not natively support SQL Server firewall rule change logging in CloudWatch without custom configurations. The automation suggested by the first AI opinion assumes pre-existing configurations that are not inherently supported by AWS, as noted by the second AI opinion. Therefore, manual verification is necessary to ensure compliance.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. Navigate to the AWS Management Console and access the RDS service. 2. Review the configuration of RDS SQL Server instances to determine if any custom logging or metric filters have been set up to capture firewall rule changes. 3. If custom logging is configured, verify that CloudWatch Logs contain entries for SQL Server firewall rule changes. 4. Check CloudWatch Alarms to ensure there are alarms configured to trigger on these log entries. 5. Confirm that the alarms are set to notify the appropriate personnel or systems when a firewall rule change occurs.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit field contains detailed instructions on how to verify the presence of the Activity Log Alert.
- The audit mentions Azure CLI commands (`az monitor activity-log alert list`) which can query the required properties.
- The Azure SDK for Python also provides methods to interact with the Activity Log Alerts, confirming that this control can be automated.
- The types of changes being monitored (SQL Server firewall rules) and their relation to standard monitoring practices in Azure further support that this is a technical compliance check rather than a policy-based one. 

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_monitor_activity_log_alert_sql_server_firewall_rule_exists

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use Azure CLI commands to check the existence of Activity Log Alert rules:
  - `az monitor activity-log alert list --subscription <subscription_id> --query "[?contains(condition.allOf[*], 'Microsoft.Sql/servers/firewallRules/write')]"`  
    - This command will filter the activity log alerts to see if there is one that monitors for SQL Server firewall rule changes.
- Use Azure SDK for Python:
  - Import `azure.mgmt.monitor` and utilize the `ActivityLogAlertsOperations` class to call the `list` method.
- Check specific attributes:
  - Validate that the output includes conditions where `operationName` is equal to `Microsoft.Sql/servers/firewallRules/write`.
  - Confirm that an action group is assigned for notifications.
- Clear Pass/Fail criteria:
  - Pass: The response includes an alert with the specified operation name and has an action group defined.
  - Fail: The response does not include any alerts that meet these criteria or does not notify through an action group.
